---
title: "Dissonance in Contra Dance Tunes"
author: "Jennifer England"
format: 
  dashboard:
    theme: yeti
embed-resources: true
---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| message: false


# functions needed to run
library(tidyverse)

```


# Introduction
::: {.card}
Contra dance is a "cousin" of English Country Dance and square dance, and is a popular form of social dance in America, Canada, and parts of Europe. Contra is a "called" dance, meaning that a person in the role of the "caller" prompts each piece of the choreography in time with the music. It is also a social dance, where dancers pair up with different partners for each dance and interact with many other dancers in the course of a given dance.

Contra dance choreographies have a fairly strict structure, which assumes there are a total of 4 sections of equal length. The first 2 sections are the same (or nearly the same), and the last 2 sections are also the same (or nearly the same). For ease of reference, musicians, callers, and dancers usually refer to these as "A" and "B" sections. So, the four sections of the dance are usually A, A, B, B (or A, A1, B, B1 if there are slight variations in the repetition of each of the main sections). As a dance progresses, the dancers loop through this same structure at least 8-10 times (dependent on the number of dancers present).

![](choreo_graph.png){width=400}

Because of this fairly strict structure, I'm curious about the musical structure of the tunes that go along with these choreographies. This project is part of a larger study I'm working on that examines 934 tunes (drawn from *The Portland Collection*, a well-known tune book that includes both traditional and modern tunes). The dataset used in this project was previously created by using a symbolic music notation system (Humdrum) to encode the dances in a way that makes them easily searchable *en masse*, so I can then pull out and analyze various musical information about them.

The underlying hypothesis of this project, based on participant-observation experience, is that the "B" sections of the music are more likely to contain energy-generating characteristics. Within the cycle of the dance, this would serve to create an extra increase of energy as we approach the "top" of the tune again and then begin repeating the cycle. One characteristic which can serve to create tension (and therefore energy) in musical contexts is musical dissonance. For this specific project, I'm focusing on the melody of the tunes and looking at the differences between how A vs. B sections treat musical dissonance. When we think about musical dissonance we are usually thinking about melody notes (the main part of the tune) and how they relate to the "back-up" parts (the accompaniment).

This study will use scale-degree numbers to refer to individual pitches within the various tunes. When we think of musical scales, we often simply number the consecutive pitches by a given point of reference, based on the starting point of the scale. The numbers will run from 1 to 7 and then start over, as shown below:

![](dissonance_score_example_0001.png)


We can then build chords off of each one of these notes, by stacking a total of 3 pitches, always in an every-other-pitch configuration, as shown below. If we start on note #1, we'll use pitches 1-3-5; if we start on note #2, we'll use pitches 2-4-6.

![](dissonance_score_example_chords_0002.png)



Finally, when we look at a "real" piece of music (such as the opening to the tune "Eighth of January"), we have a melody on the top and chords underneath. We can then look at the numbers and determine which notes don't "fit" with the chords. The figure below highlights the notes that don't fit by marking them in red.

![](dissonance_score_example_combined_0002.png)

Occasionally this is made more complicated by alterations to some of the notes, so that multiple versions of a particular numbers could be present. In the example below, two different forms of scale degrees 3, 5, and 7 are included. Since they still represent the same distance from the reference note, and would tend to be viewed identically in terms of their function, the presentation of data here will generally collapse all different classes of a given scale degree into a single class (in other words, all possible forms of "3" will be just labelled as a "3.")

![](dissonance_score_example_altered_tones_0002.png)



In order to look at how these tunes use dissonance, the data presented here first summarizes the melodies as a whole in terms of what scale degrees are present, and then examines how often each scale degree is used to create a dissonance. Taking this approach allows us to view how "special" the various dissonances are--for instance, if scale degree 5 shows up frequently but is rarely used as a dissonance, this is different musical information than if scale degree 5 is rare but is used as a dissonance the majority of the time.

The information about all scale degrees and then the dissonant scale degrees is examined first for all A sections as a whole, then all B sections as a whole, and then finally a pairwise comparison to see what differences exist for the A and B usages within each tune.
:::


# Complete scale degree data

## Row {height=60%}
::: {.card title="Average scale degree, A sections"}


```{r}

# Analyze "all" solfege pitches, including those that are dissonant

#read in data
SD_A=read_csv("A_SD_dur.txt", show_col_types=FALSE)

SD_B=read_csv("B_SD_dur.txt", show_col_types=FALSE)


#check for any logical values (where the whole column is blank)
SD_A <- SD_A |> 
  # mutate(Other = as.numeric(Other), `1-` = as.numeric(`1-`))
  mutate(
    across(where(is.logical), as.numeric)
  )

SD_B <- SD_B |> 
  # mutate(Other = as.numeric(Other), `1-` = as.numeric(`1-`))
  mutate(
    across(where(is.logical), as.numeric)
  )

#function to fill in zeroes
fill_zeroes = function(vec){
  replace_na({{ vec }}, 0)
}

# since NA values truly do represent a non-occurrence of an event (all of these are events being counted), replace all of the NA values with 0)


#fill all data that is numeric, since all are numeric except for the tune titles
SD_A_zeroes <- SD_A |> mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric),fill_zeroes)
    )

SD_B_zeroes <- SD_B |> mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric),fill_zeroes)
    )

#collapse columns of "families" of SD by summing 
SD_A_cleaned <- SD_A_zeroes |> 
  mutate(
    SD1 = `1` + `1+` + `1-`,
    SD2 = `2` + `2+` + `2-`,
    SD3 = `3` + `3+` + `3-`,
    SD4 = `4` + `4+`,
    SD5 = `5` + `5+` + `5-`,
    SD6 = `6` + `6+` + `6-`,
    SD7 = `7` + `7-`,
  ) |> 
  select (Tune_name, SD1, SD2, SD3, SD4, SD5, SD6, SD7, Other)

SD_B_cleaned <- SD_B_zeroes |> 
  mutate(
    SD1 = `1` + `1+` + `1-`,
    SD2 = `2` + `2+` + `2-`,
    SD3 = `3` + `3+` + `3-`,
    SD4 = `4` + `4+`,
    SD5 = `5` + `5+` + `5-`,
    SD6 = `6` + `6+` + `6-`,
    SD7 = `7` + `7-`,
  ) |> 
  select (Tune_name, SD1, SD2, SD3, SD4, SD5, SD6, SD7, Other)


#pivot longer, calculate average amount of dissonance on each scale degree
SD_A_summary <- pivot_longer(
  SD_A_cleaned,
  cols=c("SD1","SD2","SD3","SD4","SD5","SD6","SD7"),
  names_to = "scale_deg",
  values_to = "count"
  ) |> 
  group_by(scale_deg) |> 
  summarize(
    avg_SD = mean(count)
  )

SD_B_summary <- pivot_longer(
  SD_B_cleaned,
  cols=c("SD1","SD2","SD3","SD4","SD5","SD6","SD7"),
  names_to = "scale_deg",
  values_to = "count"
  ) |> 
  group_by(scale_deg) |> 
  summarize(
    avg_SD = mean(count)
  )

#create plot showing average count per scale degree
ggplot(SD_A_summary, aes(x = scale_deg, y = avg_SD)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#162960") + 
  xlab("Scale degree") +
  ylab("Mean (in sixteenth notes)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0,65) +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_SD,2)), vjust = -0.5) # vjust moves label above the bar




```
:::
::: {.card title="Average scale degree, B sections"}

```{r}
#second plot for B sections
ggplot(SD_B_summary, aes(x = scale_deg, y = avg_SD)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#162960") + 
  xlab("Scale degree") +
  ylab("Mean (in sixteenth notes)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0,65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_SD,2)), vjust = -0.5) 


  
```
:::

## Row {height=40%}

::: {.card title="Discussion"}

These graphs were created by simple averages of the A and B sections respectively, with all "types" of each scale degree collapsed into one category (as described in the Introduction). In other words, if there were instances of #3, b3, or regular 3 in a given tune, all of those are counted as just "3." Each section is treated separately here, with the average across all instances of that section. Ultimately we will look at paired data *within* each tune, but in order to first get a sense of the overall tendencies of each section, they are looked at within their sectional groupings first.

These two graphs appear to have a fairly similar distribution, which is not too surprising. SD 1 dominates both graphs, with SD 5 and then SD 3 as the next 2 "peaks." (This is unsurprising since these are the notes of the first chord of the scale, which we sometimes refer to as the "home" chord.) Though the total values are slightly different when comparing A to B, these differences are slight with the exception of SD 1. This information may prove interesting if it turns out that which scale degrees are treated as dissonance differs, since we have fairly similar distributions of complete scale degree at the outset.

(Here and throughout, the scale degrees were sliced and/or quantized to a sixteenth-note in length, so the counts here are in sixteenth-notes.)
:::



# Complete "dissonance" data

## Row {height=60%}
::: {.card title="Average dissonance by scale degree, A sections"}


```{r}
# Same process as above, but now only with the notes which were unsupported by the chords underneath them. 

#read in data
solf_dissA=read_csv("A_solf_diss.txt",show_col_types=FALSE)
solf_dissB=read_csv("B_solf_diss.txt",show_col_types=FALSE)


#fill all data that is numeric, since all are numeric except for the tune titles
solf_dissA_zeroes <- solf_dissA |> mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric),fill_zeroes)
    )
solf_dissB_zeroes <- solf_dissB |> mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric),fill_zeroes)
    )


#collapse columns into families by summing 
solf_dissA_cleaned <- solf_dissA_zeroes |> 
  mutate(
    SD3 = me + mi,
    SD4 = fa + fi,
    SD6 = la + le,
    SD7 = te + ti
  ) |> 
  rename(
    SD1 = do,
    SD2 = re,
    SD5 = so
  ) |> 
  select (Tune_name, SD1, SD2, SD3, SD4, SD5, SD6, SD7, Other)

solf_dissB_cleaned <- solf_dissB_zeroes |> 
  mutate(
    SD3 = me + mi,
    SD4 = fa + fi,
    SD6 = la + le,
    SD7 = te + ti
  ) |> 
  rename(
    SD1 = do,
    SD2 = re,
    SD5 = so
  ) |> 
  select (Tune_name, SD1, SD2, SD3, SD4, SD5, SD6, SD7, Other)

# At this point, since the "Other" column was empty for the total SD above, it's clear that there were a few inconsistent points of encoding in the data. For now, the "Other" columns are dropped, and an adjustment is made later on to account for the few cases where something was coded as "Other" for solfege but as a number for the SD. This is likely an issue of nomenclature and since only a few examples had this anomaly, for the purposes of visualization I'm going to more-or-less override it below
  
#pivot longer, calculate average amount of dissonance on each scale degree
solf_dissA_summary <- pivot_longer(
  solf_dissA_cleaned,
  cols=c("SD1","SD2","SD3","SD4","SD5","SD6","SD7"),
  names_to = "solfege",
  values_to = "count"
  ) |> 
  group_by(solfege) |> 
  summarize(
    avg_diss = mean(count)
  )

solf_dissB_summary <- pivot_longer(
  solf_dissB_cleaned,
  cols=c("SD1","SD2","SD3","SD4","SD5","SD6","SD7"),
  names_to = "solfege",
  values_to = "count"
  ) |> 
  group_by(solfege) |> 
  summarize(
    avg_diss = mean(count)
  )

#create plot showing average count of dissonance per scale degree

ggplot(solf_dissA_summary, aes(x = solfege, y = avg_diss)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#162960") + 
  xlab("Scale degree") +
  ylab("Mean (in sixteenth notes)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0,15) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_diss,2)), vjust = -0.5) 

```
:::
::: {.card title="Average dissonance by scale degree, B sections"}


```{r}
# second plot for B sections
ggplot(solf_dissB_summary, aes(x = solfege, y = avg_diss)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#162960") + 
  xlab("Scale degree") +
  ylab("Mean (in sixteenth notes)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0,15) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_diss,2)), vjust = -0.5) 
  
```

:::

## Row {height=40%}

::: {.card title="Discussion"}

Similar to the previous page, these two graphs are made by averaging the total dissonant scale degrees within each section group. These two graphs suggest some interesting points of observation, both compared to each other and compared to the previous page.

First, SD 1 and SD 5 are some of the smallest bars on each of these graphs, when they were the largest on the previous page. (SD 5 is the smallest on each, followed by SD 7, and then SD 1). SD 2, fourth largest on the previous graphs, is now the tallest in each of these graphs. It therefore appears that, at least in terms of the aggregate results, the different SD have different tendencies in terms of being used as a dissonance or not.

The comparison between the A and B sections still suggests fairly similar distributions--both peak at SD 2 and have their lowest number at SD 5, with SD 1 and SD 7 also being low. SD 2, 4, and 6 vary in terms of their exact placement but tend to have somewhat similar numbers.
:::

# Pairwise comparisons

## Row {height=60%}
::: {.card title="Average difference by scale degree (pairwise), A section-B section"}



```{r}

#joining for comparison data

#split the names of each, so I can pair them by the tune name (ignoring the rest of the filename, since it just explains the type of info in the original file)

#function to split, since they'll all have same formats
split_names = function(df, tune_name){
  df |> separate_wider_delim(
    {{ tune_name }},
    delim = ".", 
    names = c("tune_name","krn","content","filetype"),
    too_many = "merge"
  ) |> 
    select(-krn,-content,-filetype)
}
solf_dissA_join <- split_names(solf_dissA_cleaned,Tune_name)
solf_dissB_join <- split_names(solf_dissB_cleaned,Tune_name)
SD_A_join <- split_names(SD_A_cleaned,Tune_name)
SD_B_join <- split_names(SD_B_cleaned,Tune_name)

# join the two total SD dataframes and take differences of respective SD
SD_diff <- SD_A_join |> 
  inner_join(SD_B_join, by = "tune_name", suffix = c("A","B")) |> 
  mutate(
    SD1_diff = SD1A-SD1B,
    SD2_diff = SD2A-SD2B,
    SD3_diff = SD3A-SD3B,
    SD4_diff = SD4A-SD4B,
    SD5_diff = SD5A-SD5B,
    SD6_diff = SD6A-SD6B,
    SD7_diff = SD7A-SD7B,
  )

# same as above, but for the dissonances
diss_diff <- solf_dissA_join |> 
  inner_join(solf_dissB_join, by = "tune_name", suffix = c("A","B")) |> 
  mutate(
    SD1_diff = SD1A-SD1B,
    SD2_diff = SD2A-SD2B,
    SD3_diff = SD3A-SD3B,
    SD4_diff = SD4A-SD4B,
    SD5_diff = SD5A-SD5B,
    SD6_diff = SD6A-SD6B,
    SD7_diff = SD7A-SD7B,
    SDOther_diff = OtherA-OtherB
  ) |> 
  select(
    tune_name,SD1_diff,SD2_diff,SD3_diff,SD4_diff,SD5_diff,SD6_diff,SD7_diff,SDOther_diff
    )

# Ultimately, the "Other" case is omitted here for the same reasons as above

# summarizes the average differences per solfege for all (not dissonant)
SD_diff_summary <- pivot_longer(
  SD_diff,
  cols=c(
    "SD1_diff","SD2_diff","SD3_diff","SD4_diff","SD5_diff","SD6_diff","SD7_diff",
    ),
  names_to = "SD_diff",
  values_to = "count"
  ) |> 
  group_by(SD_diff) |> 
  summarize(
    avg_diff = mean(count)
  )

# summarizes the average differences per solfege for the dissonance
diss_diff_summary <- pivot_longer(
  diss_diff,
  cols=c(
    "SD1_diff","SD2_diff","SD3_diff","SD4_diff","SD5_diff","SD6_diff","SD7_diff",
    ),
  names_to = "diss_solfege",
  values_to = "count"
  ) |> 
  group_by(diss_solfege) |> 
  summarize(
    avg_diss = mean(count)
  )

# plot pairwise comparison for all SD, A-B sections
ggplot(SD_diff_summary, aes(x = SD_diff, y = avg_diff, fill = avg_diff<0)) +
  geom_col() +
  xlab("All SD") +
  ylab("Mean (in sixteenth notes)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c( "#162960", "#fada92")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")







```
:::
::: {.card title="Average dissonance difference by scale degree (pairwise), A section-B section"}


```{r}

# plot pairwise comparisons for diss SD, A-B sections
ggplot(diss_diff_summary, aes(x = diss_solfege, y = avg_diss,fill = avg_diss< 0)) +
  geom_col() + 
  xlab("Dissonant SD") +
  ylab("Mean (in sixteenth notes)") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c( "#162960", "#fada92")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")


```
:::

## Row {height=40%}

::: {.card title="Pairwise scale degree discussion"}
In this graph, a pairwise comparison first measures the difference in each scale degree (A-B) and then creates an average of those scale degree differences across all tunes. A positive (blue) value will mean that there are, on average, more of that scale degree in A vs. B, a negative (gold) value will mean that there are, on average, more of that scale degree in B vs. A, and a 0 value will mean that, on average, the scale degree presence is the same.

Perhaps unsurprisingly given our first set of graphs, the power of the "home" chord (SD 1, 3, and 5) is again visible here, creating the only positive values (though SD 5 is fairly close to 0). SD 2 and 4 are also fairly close to 0, approaching from the negative side, while SD 6 and 7 indicate a stronger lean towards the B sections.
:::

::: {.card title="Pairwise dissonance discussion"}
This graph is constructed similarly to the pairwise average differences, but examines only the scale degrees that are dissonant. As we might expect based on the first two pages and the other pairwise difference graph, we now see a very strong separation between the "home chord" and the other scale degrees. SD 1, 3, and 5, and are all negative (with SD5 being more strongly negative than the others), and SD 2, 4, and 6 are all positive. SD7 is also negative but near 0.

These results would suggest that dissonance on the "home" chord is treated differently in A vs. B sections. Or, perhaps another way of looking at it, that SD 2, 4, and 6 are more often treated as dissonances in A sections (where, perhaps, the "home" chord is more present), and SD 1, 3, and 5 are more often treated as dissonances in the B sections (where the "home" chord is less common). This has potential to align with the underlying hypothesis, that musical elements which create energy or tension are more likely to happen in the B section.

That said, most of these dissonance are relatively small, so the impact of these differences may not be statistically significant.
:::


# A look at proportions
## Row {height=40%}
::: {.card title="Motivation for study of proportions"}
Though there are certainly plenty of other ways that it might be useful to look at this data, one question that may well come to mind is whether looking at just averages is actually useful. There are 964 tunes in this dataset, but even with a reasonably large "n", it won't give a clear picture of cases where, say, SD 6 only occurred 3 times but was dissonant 100% of the time. This would enter the "average" as a count of 3, but would perhaps be more important to note that it was actually 100% dissonant.

The two graphs below again separate out each section but examine the proportion of dissonance of each scale degree relative to its presence in the individual tune, represented as a heatmap. Dark colors represent a low proportion of dissonance and light colors represent a high proportion of dissonance, compared to the total times the SD occurred in the tune itself. The tune names are suppressed on the x-axis for the sake of legibility.

The two graphs show variation in the patterns within each "row" (each horizontal band representing one SD), but in general these graphs do seem to tell a similar story to the previous: SD 1 and SD 5 are much less dissonant than SD 2, 4, 6, or 7, and SD 3 stands somewhere in between those two groups.
:::
## Row {height=60%}

::: {.card title="Proportions of dissonance, A sections"}

```{r}


# join the SD and the diss SD, calculate proportions (diss/all), fill props with zeroes if there were no SD in the first place
A_joined <- SD_A_join |> 
  inner_join(solf_dissA_join, by = "tune_name", suffix = c("_reg","_diss")) |> 
  mutate(
    SD1_prop = SD1_diss/SD1_reg,
    SD2_prop = SD2_diss/SD2_reg,
    SD3_prop = SD3_diss/SD3_reg,
    SD4_prop = SD4_diss/SD4_reg,
    SD5_prop = SD5_diss/SD5_reg,
    SD6_prop = SD6_diss/SD6_reg,
    SD7_prop = SD7_diss/SD7_reg,
   ) |> 
  mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric),fill_zeroes)
    ) |> 
  select (tune_name,SD1_prop,SD2_prop,SD3_prop,SD4_prop,SD5_prop,SD6_prop,SD7_prop)


B_joined <- SD_B_join |> 
  inner_join(solf_dissB_join, by = "tune_name", suffix = c("_reg","_diss")) |> 
  mutate(
    SD1_prop = SD1_diss/SD1_reg,
    SD2_prop = SD2_diss/SD2_reg,
    SD3_prop = SD3_diss/SD3_reg,
    SD4_prop = SD4_diss/SD4_reg,
    SD5_prop = SD5_diss/SD5_reg,
    SD6_prop = SD6_diss/SD6_reg,
    SD7_prop = SD7_diss/SD7_reg,
   ) |> 
  mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric),fill_zeroes)
    ) |> 
  select (tune_name,SD1_prop,SD2_prop,SD3_prop,SD4_prop,SD5_prop,SD6_prop,SD7_prop)

# Here is where the issue with the "Other" encoding becomes a problem, as a few isolated tunes had "proportions" of 2 because something was encoded as "Other" in the dissonance numbers and some form of SD in the "all" numbers. Since this is an error that would require going back to the original encoding, not the dataset itself, I am manually "fixing" it by setting the max proportion to 1 for the purposes of this project. I plan to go back to my data and see where the inconsistency lies for the "real" project though!

# pivot longer, summarize the proportions of dissonance per scale degree
A_prop_summary <- pivot_longer(
  A_joined,
  cols=c(
    "SD1_prop","SD2_prop","SD3_prop","SD4_prop","SD5_prop","SD6_prop","SD7_prop",
    ),
  names_to = "Diss_proportions",
  values_to = "prop_diss"
  ) |> mutate(prop_diss = ifelse(prop_diss>1,1,prop_diss))

B_prop_summary <- pivot_longer(
  B_joined,
  cols=c(
    "SD1_prop","SD2_prop","SD3_prop","SD4_prop","SD5_prop","SD6_prop","SD7_prop",
    ),
  names_to = "Diss_proportions",
  values_to = "prop_diss"
  ) |> mutate(prop_diss = ifelse(prop_diss>1,1,prop_diss))
 

# heatmap plot to show the proportion of scale degree (varying between 0 and 1), per scale degree, per tune

ggplot(A_prop_summary, aes(tune_name, Diss_proportions, fill= prop_diss)) + 
  geom_tile() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank()) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "RdPu") +
  xlab("Tunes (names suppressed)") +
  ylab("Proportion of dissonance (diss SD/total SD)") + 
  labs(fill = "Diss prop")




```
:::

::: {.card title="Proportions of dissonance, B sections"}

```{r}
#second heatmap plot, for the B section

ggplot(B_prop_summary, aes(tune_name, Diss_proportions, fill= prop_diss)) + 
  geom_tile() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank()) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "RdPu") +
  xlab("Tunes (names suppressed)") +
  ylab("Proportion of dissonance (diss SD/total SD)") +
  labs(fill = "Diss prop")
```
:::
# Pairwise proportions
## Row {height=100%}


::: {.card title="Proportions of pairwise dissonance, A-B sections"}
```{r}
# join the two together, calculate the pairwise difference of the two proportions
all_joined <- A_joined |>
  left_join(B_joined, by = join_by(tune_name),suffix = (c("A","B"))) |> 
  mutate(
    SD1_diff_prop = SD1_propA-SD1_propB,
    SD2_diff_prop = SD2_propA-SD2_propB,
    SD3_diff_prop = SD3_propA-SD3_propB,
    SD4_diff_prop = SD4_propA-SD4_propB,
    SD5_diff_prop = SD5_propA-SD5_propB,
    SD6_diff_prop = SD6_propA-SD6_propB,
    SD7_diff_prop = SD7_propA-SD7_propB,
  ) |> 
  select(
    tune_name,SD1_diff_prop,SD2_diff_prop,SD3_diff_prop,SD4_diff_prop,SD5_diff_prop,
    SD6_diff_prop,SD7_diff_prop
    )




# summarizes the average differences of each dissonant proportion, per SD
SD_diff_prop <- pivot_longer(
  all_joined,
  cols=c(
    "SD1_diff_prop","SD2_diff_prop","SD3_diff_prop","SD4_diff_prop","SD5_diff_prop",
    "SD6_diff_prop","SD7_diff_prop",
    ),
  names_to = "SD_diff_prop",
  values_to = "count"
  ) |> 
  group_by(SD_diff_prop) |> 
  summarize(
    avg_diss_prop = mean(count)
  )

# pairwise difference of dissonant proportions of each SD 
ggplot(SD_diff_prop, aes(x = SD_diff_prop, y = avg_diss_prop,fill = avg_diss_prop< 0)) +
  geom_col() + 
  xlab("Proportion of dissonance (diss SD/total SD)") +
  ylab("Mean") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c( "#162960", "#fada92")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

:::

::: {.card title="Discussion of pairwise proportions"}
Finally, we could examine a difference of these proportions, similar to the previous examination of a difference of averages. Perhaps unsurprisingly, we can note that:

* SD 1, 3, and 5 have one behavior, and SD 2, 4, 6, and 7 have a different behavior
  + The behavior of SD 7 is interesting here, though, since it had previously grouped (slightly) with SD 1, 3, and 5 on the last pairwise dissonance graph.

* The behavior of SD 1, 3, and 5 again favors these SD being more likely to be dissonant in B sections, and vice versa for the other group of SD.

* None of these proportion differences are particularly large, so, again, the impact of this effect if measured statistically may not be significant.

:::


# Discussion
## Row {height=100%}

::: {.card title="Summary"}
The data visualization shown in this project has demonstrated differences in dissonance usage in A vs. B sections of contra dance tunes. In this particular dataset, which scale degrees were used as dissonance was the primary area of focus. It was found that although the overall key profile (amount of each scale degree) was very similar in A and B sections, both total average and pairwise comparisons of tunes suggested that individual scales degree behaved differently between different sections of the tunes. In general, scale degrees 1, 3, and 5 were found to behave slightly differently in A sections vs. B sections, and this was found to be true both with averaging the pairwise differences and when accounting for the proportion of dissonant usage of a scale degree vs. the total usage of that same scale degree within a tune (measured in sixteenth notes). In all cases, these differences were relatively small in magnitude, so it remains to be seen whether these differences are statistically significant.
:::

::: {.card title="Further Resources"}

- [Country Dance and Song Society]
- [Dissertation on contra dance]
- [Article from *Journal of Mathematics and the Arts* about using style features to predict choreography composition dates]
- [Contra dances in Bozeman]
- [Humdrum toolkit (symbolic system and toolkit for computational analysis of music)]
- [The Portland Collection tunebooks]

[Country Dance and Song Society]: https://cdss.org/

[Dissertation on contra dance]: https://www.proquest.com/dissertations-theses/contra-dance-choreography-reflection-social/docview/304015962/se-2?accountid=28148


[Article from *Journal of Mathematics and the Arts* about using style features to predict choreography composition dates]: https://scholar.google.com/scholar?hl=en&as_sdt=0%2C27&q=Using+stylistic+features+to+predict+the+composition+date+of+an+American+contra+dance&btnG=

[Contra dances in Bozeman]: https://bozemanfolklore.org/contra-videos/

[Humdrum toolkit (symbolic system and toolkit for computational analysis of music)]: https://www.humdrum.org/

[The Portland Collection tunebooks]: https://www.theportlandcollection.com/



:::
